statement: HallusionBench is a human-expert–crafted benchmark for image-context reasoning that contains 346 images paired with 1129 questions.
reasoning: This specifies the benchmark’s scale and curation method, which are core, verifiable properties needed to understand and use the paper’s main contribution.
source_text: The benchmark comprises 346 im- ages paired with 1129 questions, all meticulously crafted by human experts.
content: HallusionBench is a human-expert–crafted benchmark for image-context reasoning that contains 346 images paired with 1129 questions.

statement: On HallusionBench, GPT-4V achieves 31.42% question-pair accuracy, while all other evaluated models score below 16% question-pair accuracy (across 15 benchmarked models).
reasoning: This is a key quantitative result establishing benchmark difficulty and a large performance gap between GPT-4V and other LVLMs under the paper’s stated metric (question-pair accuracy).
source_text: In our evaluation on  H ALLUSION - B ENCH , we benchmarked 15 different models, highlighting a 31.42% question-pair accuracy achieved by the state-of- the-art GPT-4V. Notably, all other evaluated models achieve accuracy below 16%.
content: On HallusionBench, GPT-4V achieves 31.42% question-pair accuracy, while all other evaluated models score below 16% question-pair accuracy (across 15 benchmarked models).

statement: HallusionBench contains approximately 1,129 handcrafted visual question-answer (VQA) pairs built from 165 original images and 181 human-professionally modified images.
reasoning: This is a core, concrete specification of the benchmark’s scale and its inclusion of human-edited images, which underpins its diagnostic purpose.
source_text: H ALLU - SION B ENCH consists of approximately 1129 handcrafted visual question-answer (VQA) pairs, featuring 165 origi- nal images and 181 images expertly modified by human professionals.
content: HallusionBench contains approximately 1,129 handcrafted visual question-answer (VQA) pairs built from 165 original images and 181 human-professionally modified images.

statement: On HallusionBench, GPT-4V achieves 31.42% Question Pair Accuracy, and all other evaluated methods score below 16% Question Pair Accuracy (across 15 recent methods evaluated).
reasoning: This quantitative result summarizes the benchmark’s difficulty and establishes a key comparative performance claim across multiple LVLM methods using a specific metric.
source_text: We evaluate 15 most recent methods on H ALLUSION - B ENCH . ... Notably, the SoTA GPT-4V achieves merely a 31.42% Question Pair Accuracy, while the per- formance of all other methods falls below 16%.
content: On HallusionBench, GPT-4V achieves 31.42% Question Pair Accuracy, and all other evaluated methods score below 16% Question Pair Accuracy (across 15 recent methods evaluated).

statement: HALLUSIONBENCH contains 1129 human-edited question–answer pairs and 346 total images, of which 181 are human-edited, and it is constructed as image/video image-pairs enabling control-group analysis.
reasoning: This captures the benchmark’s core methodological contribution (paired, manually edited images for control-group analysis) and its concrete scale (QA and image counts), which are key for understanding what is novel about the dataset.
source_text: H ALLUSION B ENCH Image, Video Image Pairs 1129 1129 346 181 ✓ ... Table 1. ... H ALLUSION B ENCH is the first and the only benchmark that focuses on control-group analysis by carefully editing each image in the database manually.
content: HALLUSIONBENCH contains 1129 human-edited question–answer pairs and 346 total images, of which 181 are human-edited, and it is constructed as image/video image-pairs enabling control-group analysis.

statement: In HALLUSIONBENCH, 45% of questions use human-edited visual inputs, 39% use original online images, and 16% are evaluated without any visual input.
reasoning: This quantifies how much of the benchmark explicitly tests robustness to human edits versus standard images or text-only settings, which is central to the benchmark’s evaluation design.
source_text: Fig. 2 ( right ) describes the distribution of the questions without visual input (16%), with original online images (39%), and with visual input edited by human experts (45%).
content: In HALLUSIONBENCH, 45% of questions use human-edited visual inputs, 39% use original online images, and 16% are evaluated without any visual input.

statement: The paper introduces three quantitative analytical evaluation criteria—Yes/No Bias Test, Consistency Test, and Diagnostic Test—to measure and diagnose LVLM failures, and states they are ordered by increasing complexity such that later tests are less useful if earlier basic tests fail.
reasoning: This is a core methodological contribution: a structured, staged set of quantitative diagnostics for LVLM failure analysis, including an explicit dependency/ordering among tests.
source_text: “we introduce three analyt- ical criteria to measure and diagnose the failures of LVLMs, Yes/No Bias Test ,  Consistency Test , and  Diagnostic Test . ... These tests are listed in the order of complexity, so the latter test would not be as useful and insightful if the former basic test failed.”
content: The paper introduces three quantitative analytical evaluation criteria—Yes/No Bias Test, Consistency Test, and Diagnostic Test—to measure and diagnose LVLM failures, and states they are ordered by increasing complexity such that later tests are less useful if earlier basic tests fail.

statement: To reduce variance in GPT-4 judging (despite temperature set to 0), the paper evaluates each LVLM output three times with GPT-4 and reports the average score, and reports that GPT-4-assisted evaluation shows negligible difference from human evaluation for GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 (as referenced in Tables 2 and 3).
reasoning: This is an important evaluation-design claim: it specifies a concrete protocol (3 runs + averaging) to address judge variance and asserts empirical alignment with human labels on specific models.
source_text: “It is found that outputs of GPT- 4 still exist variance, although the temperature is set as 0. Therefore, we utilize GPT-4 to evaluate the outputs of LLMs 3 times and report average scores.”; “As shown in the first two rows of Tab.  2  and Tab.  3 , the negligible differ- ence proves that the GPT4-assisted method aligns well with human judgment.”
content: To reduce variance in GPT-4 judging (despite temperature set to 0), the paper evaluates each LVLM output three times with GPT-4 and reports the average score, and reports that GPT-4-assisted evaluation shows negligible difference from human evaluation for GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 (as referenced in Tables 2 and 3).

statement: On HALLUSIONBENCH (Table 2), under GPT4-assisted evaluation, GPT-4V achieves 65.28% overall accuracy (aAcc), outperforming Claude 3 (56.86%) and BLIP2-T5 (48.09%).
reasoning: This is a key quantitative leaderboard result establishing the strongest baseline model and the margin over other prominent LVLMs on the paper’s benchmark.
source_text: Table 2. ... GPT4V ... GPT4-Assisted ... All Accuracy ( aAcc ) ↑ ... 65.28 ... Claude 3 ... GPT4-Assisted ... 56.86 ... BLIP2-T5 ... GPT4-Assisted ... 48.09
content: On HALLUSIONBENCH (Table 2), under GPT4-assisted evaluation, GPT-4V achieves 65.28% overall accuracy (aAcc), outperforming Claude 3 (56.86%) and BLIP2-T5 (48.09%).

statement: On HALLUSIONBENCH (Table 2), under GPT4-assisted evaluation, Qwen-VL’s overall accuracy (aAcc=39.15%) is lower than the Random Chance baseline (aAcc=45.96%).
reasoning: This is a non-obvious, counterintuitive quantitative finding showing a named LVLM underperforms random guessing on the benchmark’s overall accuracy metric.
source_text: Table 2. ... Qwen-VL ... GPT4-Assisted ... All Accuracy ( aAcc ) ↑ ... 39.15 ... Random Chance ... GPT4-Assisted ... All Accuracy ( aAcc ) ↑ ... 45.96
content: On HALLUSIONBENCH (Table 2), under GPT4-assisted evaluation, Qwen-VL’s overall accuracy (aAcc=39.15%) is lower than the Random Chance baseline (aAcc=45.96%).

statement: In the paper’s decision-tree diagnosis for a visual-supplement (VS) sample (Fig. 35 bottom; route (VS) B–R–G), LLaVA-1.5 produces an incorrect answer (score 0) both without any image and with the original supplementary image, and the two answers are identical; this is diagnosed as Language Hallucination.
reasoning: This is a concrete, diagnostic-method-specific finding showing that adding the image does not change correctness or content, supporting the paper’s hallucination categorization.
source_text: In Fig. 35 (bottom), it is a visual-supplement sample (VS). The answer is incorrect (0) without being given any image. The answer is still incorrect (0) when the original supplementary image is given. And the two answers are the same. This shows that LLaVA-1.5 has the issue of hallucinations with and without the image information.
content: In the paper’s decision-tree diagnosis for a visual-supplement (VS) sample (Fig. 35 bottom; route (VS) B–R–G), LLaVA-1.5 produces an incorrect answer (score 0) both without any image and with the original supplementary image, and the two answers are identical; this is diagnosed as Language Hallucination.

statement: In the paper’s Figure 7 (Visual Dependent – Optical Illusion of Size), GPT-4V answers “Yes” to the Chubb-illusion question about whether the center areas are identical even after the image is edited so the left circle is enlarged (ground-truth answer: “No”), indicating GPT-4V does not update its response based on the edited visual context.
reasoning: This is a concrete failure case showing that GPT-4V’s response remains tied to a known illusion description rather than the actual (edited) image content.
source_text: Edited:  The circle on the left are enlarged. Answer:  No, they are not identical. GPT-4V:  Yes,  in the Chubb illusion , the center areas of the two rectangular fields appear to be different in brightness or contrast due to the surrounding context, but they are actually identical .
content: In the paper’s Figure 7 (Visual Dependent – Optical Illusion of Size), GPT-4V answers “Yes” to the Chubb-illusion question about whether the center areas are identical even after the image is edited so the left circle is enlarged (ground-truth answer: “No”), indicating GPT-4V does not update its response based on the edited visual context.

statement: The paper claims (Figure 7) that GPT-4V tends to answer optical-illusion prompts using parametric memory of well-known illusions rather than the actual visual context, and that this behavior persists even on hand-crafted edited examples that did not appear before.
reasoning: This is a core methodological/behavioral finding about the model’s failure mode (memory-driven responding) and its generalization to novel, hand-crafted counterexamples.
source_text: GPT-4V tends to generate answers based on its parametric memory of existing well-known optical illusions instead of the actual visual context. Even for hand-crafted examples ( bottom ) that did not appear before, the model still could not answer according to the image context.
content: The paper claims (Figure 7) that GPT-4V tends to answer optical-illusion prompts using parametric memory of well-known illusions rather than the actual visual context, and that this behavior persists even on hand-crafted edited examples that did not appear before.

statement: In the paper’s Figure 8 (Visual Dependent – Optical Illusion of Length), GPT-4V answers that two yellow lines are the same length even when the image is edited so that the bottom yellow line is elongated (ground-truth answer: the bottom line is longer).
reasoning: This is a concrete failure case showing that GPT-4V can be misled by an edited length-illusion setup, which is central to evaluating robustness to visual manipulations.
source_text: Prompt:  Are the two yellow lines the same length? Edited:  The bottom yellow line is elongated. Answer:  No, the bottom yellow line is longer. GPT-4V:  Yes, the two yellow lines appear to be  the same length.
content: In the paper’s Figure 8 (Visual Dependent – Optical Illusion of Length), GPT-4V answers that two yellow lines are the same length even when the image is edited so that the bottom yellow line is elongated (ground-truth answer: the bottom line is longer).

statement: The paper states (Figure 8 caption/description) that both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 perform poorly at recognizing and measuring length in optical-illusion-style images, and that GPT-4V is easily tricked by the scene/setup despite recognizing many illusion cases.
reasoning: This summarizes a key empirical finding about model limitations on a specific visual reasoning skill (length measurement) under illusion contexts.
source_text: GPT-4V can recognize many optical illusion cases but is also easily tricked by the scene and setup of the images. Both models have bad performance in recognizing and measuring length.
content: The paper states (Figure 8 caption/description) that both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 perform poorly at recognizing and measuring length in optical-illusion-style images, and that GPT-4V is easily tricked by the scene/setup despite recognizing many illusion cases.

statement: In the paper’s Figure 9 (visual-illusion evaluation), GPT-4V correctly identifies the Hermann grid illusion but still answers that there are no grey dots at the intersections even when the edited image explicitly adds grey points, indicating a failure to answer based on the actual image content.
reasoning: This is a key, non-obvious behavioral finding: the model’s illusion recognition does not guarantee image-grounded correctness, and the edited-image condition makes the failure independently verifiable.
source_text: Edited:  Grey points are added. Answer:  Yes. GPT-4V:  No,  there are no grey dots in the intersections.  The optical illusion you’re referencing is known as the  Hermann grid illusion ... Figure 9. ... GPT-4V recognizes the illusion cases but fails to answer the question faithfully based on the actual image context.
content: In the paper’s Figure 9 (visual-illusion evaluation), GPT-4V correctly identifies the Hermann grid illusion but still answers that there are no grey dots at the intersections even when the edited image explicitly adds grey points, indicating a failure to answer based on the actual image content.

statement: In the paper’s Figure 9 (Torsion dystonia illusion prompt), GPT-4V answers that the arcs come from circles with the same diameter even when the edited image changes the ground truth to different diameters, while LLaVA-1.5 answers 'not the same' in both original and edited cases.
reasoning: This contrast across original vs edited ground truth isolates a robustness issue: GPT-4V appears anchored to the illusion prior, whereas LLaVA-1.5 tracks the (edited) factual label more consistently.
source_text: Prompt:  According to the Torsion dystonia illusion, are the diameters of the three circles the same? ... Original Answer:  Yes ... GPT-4V:  Yes ... LLaVA-1.5:  No ... Edited:  The curves have different diameters. Answer:  No ... GPT-4V:  Yes,  all three arcs are part of circles with the same diameter. ... LLaVA-1.5:  No, the diameters ... are not the same.
content: In the paper’s Figure 9 (Torsion dystonia illusion prompt), GPT-4V answers that the arcs come from circles with the same diameter even when the edited image changes the ground truth to different diameters, while LLaVA-1.5 answers 'not the same' in both original and edited cases.

statement: In Figure 10 (page 15), for a geometry prompt about whether angle 1 + angle 2 equals 180 degrees under the parallel lines theorem, both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 answer “Yes” on the original image and still answer “Yes” on an edited image whose ground-truth answer is “No,” indicating failure to recognize whether the depicted lines are actually parallel.
reasoning: This isolates a key failure mode: models apply memorized theorem knowledge but do not verify the visual precondition (parallelism), leading to incorrect answers under image edits.
source_text: “Original image Answer: Yes. GPT-4V: Yes… LLaVA-1.5: Yes… Edited image Answer: No. GPT-4V: Yes… LLaVA-1.5: Yes… Figure 10… Top: GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 can memorize famous mathematical theorems but are unable to recognize the correct parallel lines in the image.”
content: In Figure 10 (page 15), for a geometry prompt about whether angle 1 + angle 2 equals 180 degrees under the parallel lines theorem, both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 answer “Yes” on the original image and still answer “Yes” on an edited image whose ground-truth answer is “No,” indicating failure to recognize whether the depicted lines are actually parallel.

statement: In Figure 10 (page 15), for the prompt “Are the two red vertical lines both straight?”, GPT-4V answers “Yes” on both the original image (ground-truth “Yes”) and an edited image (ground-truth “No”), while LLaVA-1.5 answers “Yes” on the original and “No” on the edited image.
reasoning: This provides a concrete, model-specific comparison showing GPT-4V’s geometry-recognition failure under visual edits, while LLaVA-1.5 changes its answer in line with the edited ground truth.
source_text: “Prompt: Are the two red vertical lines both straight? Original image Answer: Yes. GPT-4V: Yes… LLaVA-1.5: Yes… Edited image Answer: No. GPT-4V: Yes… LLaVA-1.5: No… Bottom: GPT-4V is unable to distinguish whether two lines are straight.”
content: In Figure 10 (page 15), for the prompt “Are the two red vertical lines both straight?”, GPT-4V answers “Yes” on both the original image (ground-truth “Yes”) and an edited image (ground-truth “No”), while LLaVA-1.5 answers “Yes” on the original and “No” on the edited image.

statement: In Figure 11 (Geometry&Math visual-dependent prompts), when the triangle image is edited so that the shape is no longer a triangle, both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 still answer “Yes” to the question “Is angleA + angleB + angleC = 180?” even though the edited-image ground-truth answer is “No.”
reasoning: This is a concrete failure case showing that both models rely on a memorized geometric rule rather than recognizing that the edited visual input violates the triangle assumption.
source_text: Edited image Answer:  No. GPT-4V:  Yes, the sum of the interior angles of a triangle always equals 180 degrees. So,  angleA + angleB +angleC = 180. LLaVA-1.5:  Yes,  angleA + angleB + angleC = 180... For example, the edited image in the  Top  is obviously not a triangle
content: In Figure 11 (Geometry&Math visual-dependent prompts), when the triangle image is edited so that the shape is no longer a triangle, both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 still answer “Yes” to the question “Is angleA + angleB + angleC = 180?” even though the edited-image ground-truth answer is “No.”

statement: In Figure 11, when the triangle image is edited so that it is no longer a right triangle, both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 still answer “Yes” to the question “Is the value of x in the image 5?” (using right-triangle/Pythagorean reasoning), despite the edited-image ground-truth answer being “No.”
reasoning: This isolates a key methodological limitation: the models apply a standard theorem-based explanation even when the visual evidence contradicts the theorem’s preconditions.
source_text: Edited image Answer:  No. GPT-4V:  To determine the value of x in the triangle, we can use the Pythagorean theorem for right triangles... so, yes, the value of x in the image is 5. LLaVA-1.5:  Yes,  the value of x in the image is 5... the edited image in the  Bottom  is obviously not a right triangle.
content: In Figure 11, when the triangle image is edited so that it is no longer a right triangle, both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 still answer “Yes” to the question “Is the value of x in the image 5?” (using right-triangle/Pythagorean reasoning), despite the edited-image ground-truth answer being “No.”

statement: In Figure 12’s OCR-based visual-dependent prompts, GPT-4V answers “Yes” to “Does the image show ‘Beijing Roast Duck’?” even when the edited image’s ground-truth answer is “No,” indicating it relies on the well-known association rather than the edited visual/text context.
reasoning: This is a concrete failure case showing the model’s OCR/visual grounding can be overridden by prior knowledge, which is central to the paper’s analysis of hallucination/illusion behaviors.
source_text: Edited image Answer:  No. GPT-4V:  Yes, the image appears to  depict "Beijing Roast Duck" ... The Chinese characters at the top of the image also confirm this as they read "Beijing Roast Duck."
content: In Figure 12’s OCR-based visual-dependent prompts, GPT-4V answers “Yes” to “Does the image show ‘Beijing Roast Duck’?” even when the edited image’s ground-truth answer is “No,” indicating it relies on the well-known association rather than the edited visual/text context.

statement: Figure 12 reports that for edited advertisements where the region associated with a famous dish is modified, both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 still respond with the dish’s canonical region (e.g., Wuhan for hot dry noodles), ignoring the edited context.
reasoning: This summarizes a cross-model comparative finding: both models exhibit the same systematic error pattern under region-edited stimuli, which is a key non-obvious behavioral claim.
source_text: We highlight several advertisements with famous regional dishes with modifications on the regions. In both cases, GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 ignore the context and still reply with the well-known regions for that food.
content: Figure 12 reports that for edited advertisements where the region associated with a famous dish is modified, both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 still respond with the dish’s canonical region (e.g., Wuhan for hot dry noodles), ignoring the edited context.

statement: In Figure 13 (top) of paper 2935bdfb13a1 (page 18), when one of Xuanzang’s disciples in an image is replaced by a girl, both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 still answer that Xuanzang is accompanied by three disciples, indicating their judgments are influenced by parametric memory/stereotyped associations rather than the edited visual evidence.
reasoning: This is a concrete failure case showing that the models’ outputs can be dominated by memorized narrative priors ("Journey to the West") even when the visual content is edited to contradict that prior.
source_text: Edited: One of the disciples is replaced. Answer: No. GPT-4V: Yes... LLaVA-1.5: Yes... Figure 13... Top: The judgments of GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 are affected by parametric memory and stereotyped judgment, meaning that they do not recognize that the girl is not Xuanzang’s disciple.
content: In Figure 13 (top) of paper 2935bdfb13a1 (page 18), when one of Xuanzang’s disciples in an image is replaced by a girl, both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 still answer that Xuanzang is accompanied by three disciples, indicating their judgments are influenced by parametric memory/stereotyped associations rather than the edited visual evidence.

statement: In the Figure 15 multiple-images temporal-order prompts, GPT-4V gives the same answer for both the original and reversed image sequences, indicating it does not distinguish between positive and reversed temporal order (e.g., it answers “Yes” for the positive-sequence question even when the reversed-sequence ground-truth answer is “No”).
reasoning: This is a concrete behavioral failure case: reversing the image order flips the labeled answer, but GPT-4V’s response remains aligned with the original sequence, evidencing insensitivity to temporal order.
source_text: Reversed sequence Answer:  No. GPT-4V:  In the positive sequence of the images, the plug is  put into the power outlet . ... Reversed sequence Answer:  No. GPT-4V:  Yes, according to the sequence of the images, it appears that the baby is attempting to  put on the clothes . ... Reversed sequence Answer:  No. GPT-4V:  Yes, according to the positive sequence of the images,  the temperature increases .
content: In the Figure 15 multiple-images temporal-order prompts, GPT-4V gives the same answer for both the original and reversed image sequences, indicating it does not distinguish between positive and reversed temporal order (e.g., it answers “Yes” for the positive-sequence question even when the reversed-sequence ground-truth answer is “No”).

statement: Based on the Figure 15 examples, the authors conclude that GPT-4V lacks temporal reasoning ability for multiple-image prompts and cannot distinguish positive versus reversed image sequences even when they depict different actions.
reasoning: This is the paper’s explicit takeaway from the provided examples, summarizing a key limitation relevant to the method’s capabilities.
source_text: From the examples, we found that GPT-4V lacks the ability of temporal reasoning and is unable to distinguish between the positive sequence and reversed sequence of the images, even though they represent different actions.
content: Based on the Figure 15 examples, the authors conclude that GPT-4V lacks temporal reasoning ability for multiple-image prompts and cannot distinguish positive versus reversed image sequences even when they depict different actions.

content: Given the chart as input, both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 fail to extract the correct information and instead answer that China, Hong Kong SAR is the leading importer, which the paper categorizes as an incorrect answer due to visual illusion, language hallucination, or potentially mixed causes.

statement: In the Figure 17 chart-based QA example (UK exports of bread/biscuits/wafers/cakes/pastries, 2001–2019), GPT-4V answers the prompt correctly that 2010 is not the peak export year and that the highest export value occurs in 2019.

statement: In the paper’s Figure 18 chart-based prompt about China’s 15–29 population (2000–2050), GPT-4V avoids hallucination when uncertain without a chart, but when given the chart image it answers incorrectly, indicating a failure to correctly interpret the chart.
reasoning: This isolates a key empirical finding about GPT-4V’s behavior difference between text-only uncertainty handling and visual chart understanding, which is central to evaluating multimodal reliability.

statement: In the paper’s Figure 18 example, LLaVA-1.5 produces the same answer with and without the image context, which the authors attribute to reliance on parametric memory rather than visual grounding.

statement: In Figure 19’s edited (contradictory) map prompts, both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 answer based on parametric memory rather than the provided visual context, leading them to misinterpret the edited map.

statement: For the Figure 19 map prompt 'Is New Mexico state larger than Texas state?' with an edited image where New Mexico and Texas are swapped, LLaVA-1.5 outputs 'No' in its response text but the recorded final 'Answer' is 'Yes'.

content: For the Figure 19 map prompt 'Is New Mexico state larger than Texas state?' with an edited image where New Mexico and Texas are swapped, LLaVA-1.5 outputs 'No' in its response text but the recorded final 'Answer' is 'Yes'.

source_text: “Both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 have an affirmative and correct answer without context… However, both models fail to answer the question correctly based on the data provided in the chart.”
content: In Figure 22’s chart-prompt example, both GPT-4V and LLaVA-1.5 respond affirmatively that China has the largest population followed by India and then the USA, but the paper states both answers are incorrect when judged against the chart data.

statement: The FAITHSCORE metric is a reference-free, fine-grained evaluation method that measures the faithfulness of large vision-language models' (LVLMs) generated answers by extracting atomic facts from sub-sentences and verifying their consistency with the input image.
reasoning: This fact captures the novel methodological contribution of the paper, specifying the unique steps of the FAITHSCORE metric and its reference-free, atomic fact-based approach to evaluating LVLM outputs.
source_text: We introduce F AITH S CORE ( Faith fulness to Atomic Image Facts Score ), a reference-free and fine-grained evaluation metric that measures the faithfulness of the generated free-form answers from large vision-language models (LVLMs). The F AITH S CORE evaluation first identifies sub-sentences containing descriptive statements that need to be verified, then extracts a comprehensive list of atomic facts from these sub-sentences, and finally conducts consistency verification between fine-grained atomic facts and the input image.
content: The FAITHSCORE metric is a reference-free, fine-grained evaluation method that measures the faithfulness of large vision-language models' (LVLMs) generated answers by extracting atomic facts from sub-sentences and verifying their consistency with the input image.

statement: Meta-evaluation demonstrates that the FAITHSCORE metric highly correlates with human judgments of faithfulness in LVLM-generated answers.
reasoning: This fact is important because it provides evidence for the validity of the proposed metric, showing that it aligns well with human evaluation, which is a key requirement for any automatic evaluation metric.
source_text: Meta-evaluation demon- strates that our metric highly correlates with human judgments of faithfulness.
content: Meta-evaluation demonstrates that the FAITHSCORE metric highly correlates with human judgments of faithfulness in LVLM-generated answers.

statement: The FAITHSCORE metric is proposed to evaluate fine-grained hallucinations in LVLMs' free-form answers for various multimodal tasks, using three components: Descriptive Sub-sentence Identification, Atomic Fact Generation, and Fact Verification via a Visual Entailment Model.
reasoning: This fact is central to the paper's methodological contribution, describing the novel metric and its components, which is not common knowledge and requires understanding the proposed approach.
source_text: To address the aforementioned challenges, we propose the FAITHSCORE metric, which can evaluate fine-grained hallucinations in various multimodal tasks, such as image captioning and open-ended questions. This metric comprises three primary components: Descriptive Sub-sentence Identification, Atomic Fact Generation, and Fact Verification, as illustrated in Figure 2. ... The last component emphasizes verifying the consistency between the visual information and the derived atomic facts via a Visual Entailment Model (VEM) ( Xie et al. ,  2019 ).
content: The FAITHSCORE metric is proposed to evaluate fine-grained hallucinations in LVLMs' free-form answers for various multimodal tasks, using three components: Descriptive Sub-sentence Identification, Atomic Fact Generation, and Fact Verification via a Visual Entailment Model.

statement: Evaluation using the FAITHSCORE metric shows that advanced LVLMs such as LLaVA and MiniGPT-4 still generate answers that are not fully faithful to the input image, indicating significant room for improvement.
reasoning: This fact summarizes a key empirical finding of the paper, showing the practical impact of the proposed metric and the current limitations of state-of-the-art models, which is important for researchers assessing LVLM reliability.
source_text: Based on the proposed metric, we evaluated several advanced LVLMs, such as LLaVA ( Liu et al. , 2023e ) and MiniGPT-4 ( Zhu et al. , 2023 ). From the results, we conclude that current LVLMs still face challenges of answers that are not faithful to the input image, which leaves a large room for improvement.
content: Evaluation using the FAITHSCORE metric shows that advanced LVLMs such as LLaVA and MiniGPT-4 still generate answers that are not fully faithful to the input image, indicating significant room for improvement.

statement: The paper introduces a reference-free metric for fine-grained evaluation of answers in open-ended visual question-answering, where answers can be of free form and lengthy.
reasoning: This is a novel methodological claim central to the paper's contribution, distinguishing their approach from prior work that relies on references or is limited to constrained settings.
source_text: Different from all the above, we are the first to propose   a   reference-free   metric   for   fine-grained evaluating   the   answers   in   the   open-ended   visual question-answering setting, where answers are of free form and can be lengthy.
content: The paper introduces a reference-free metric for fine-grained evaluation of answers in open-ended visual question-answering, where answers can be of free form and lengthy.

statement: The proposed FAITHSCORE evaluation framework consists of three steps: descriptive sub-sentence identification, atomic fact generation, and fact verification.
reasoning: This fact details the methodological innovation and is essential for understanding how the proposed metric operates, which is critical for researchers evaluating or building upon this work.
source_text: In order to estimate F AITH S CORE of the generated answers,   we   introduce   a   novel   framework   to   im- plement   the   scoring   function   F .   The   framework comprises three key steps: descriptive sub-sentence identification, atomic fact generation, and fact veri- fication, as depicted in Figure  2 .
content: The proposed FAITHSCORE evaluation framework consists of three steps: descriptive sub-sentence identification, atomic fact generation, and fact verification.

statement: The proposed method uses ChatGPT as both a recognizer to identify descriptive sub-sentences and a decomposer to generate atomic facts from these sub-sentences, enabling fine-grained evaluation of LVLM-generated answers.
reasoning: This fact synthesizes the methodological core: leveraging ChatGPT in two roles (recognizer and decomposer) to automate and fine-grain the process of extracting and evaluating atomic facts, which is central to the paper's contribution.
source_text: To be more specific, our approach first crafts a prompt P that encompasses task instructions and K1 in-context learning examples. We feed this designed prompt along with the to-be-processed answer A into the ChatGPT, generating the recognized results... Then we extract all descriptive sub-sentences... we design a decomposer to further break the sub-sentences into atomic facts... we also utilize the ChatGPT for the generation of atomic facts.
content: The proposed method uses ChatGPT as both a recognizer to identify descriptive sub-sentences and a decomposer to generate atomic facts from these sub-sentences, enabling fine-grained evaluation of LVLM-generated answers.

statement: ChatGPT is used to automate the identification of descriptive sub-sentences in LVLM-generated answers, addressing the resource-intensive nature of manual annotation and achieving promising performance as shown in Section 4.2.
reasoning: This fact highlights a key practical innovation: automating a previously labor-intensive annotation step using ChatGPT, which is important for scalability and reproducibility of the evaluation pipeline.
source_text: Humans are capable of distinguishing descriptive sub-sentences from other contents... However, manually identifying descriptive sub-sentences is a resource-intensive process... we turn to ChatGPT to implement the recognizer as a practical solution, since it has demonstrated remarkable semantics understanding capabilities... Section 4.2 shows that ChatGPT can achieve promising performance on this task.
content: ChatGPT is used to automate the identification of descriptive sub-sentences in LVLM-generated answers, addressing the resource-intensive nature of manual annotation and achieving promising performance as shown in Section 4.2.

statement: ChatGPT achieves an accuracy of 89.84% overall and 91.58% on the 'color', 'count', and 'others' categories in identifying descriptive sub-sentences, outperforming both LLaMA-7B (68.01% overall) and LLaMA-7B with context (72.80% overall) on the same task.
reasoning: This fact quantitatively compares the performance of different LLMs on a specific sub-sentence recognition task, highlighting ChatGPT's superior accuracy. It requires interpreting table data and understanding the evaluation context.
source_text: ChatGPT 89.84 91.58 90.74 LLaMA-7B 68.01 71.39 69.75 LLaMA-7B (w/ context) 72.80 66.76 69.68 Table 1: Comparison of recognizer LLMs’ accuracy (%) on identifying descriptive sub-sentences.
content: ChatGPT achieves an accuracy of 89.84% overall and 91.58% on the 'color', 'count', and 'others' categories in identifying descriptive sub-sentences, outperforming both LLaMA-7B (68.01% overall) and LLaMA-7B with context (72.80% overall) on the same task.

statement: Among all evaluated visual entailment models (VEMs), LLaVA-1.5 achieves the highest accuracy on atomic fact verification, with scores of 85.65%, 84.49%, and 85.07% across three metrics.
reasoning: This fact is meaningful because it quantitatively identifies the best-performing model for atomic fact verification, which is central to the paper's methodology for evaluating LVLM faithfulness. The claim is supported by accuracy values in Table 2 and directly impacts the choice of model for subsequent analyses.
source_text: Among all models, LLaVA-1.5 performs best on fact verification, so we use it for estimating FAITHSCORE in Section 5.
content: Among all evaluated visual entailment models (VEMs), LLaVA-1.5 achieves the highest accuracy on atomic fact verification, with scores of 85.65%, 84.49%, and 85.07% across three metrics.

statement: The proposed FAITHSCORE metric shows the highest correlation with human judgment of LVLM hallucinations among all evaluated metrics, with statistical significance (p < 0.01) compared to baselines.
reasoning: This fact is important because it demonstrates the superiority of the proposed evaluation metric (FAITHSCORE) over established alternatives, using quantitative correlation with human ratings and statistical significance. This directly supports the paper's core claim about the effectiveness of their metric.
source_text: Table 3 delineates the correlation between various evaluation metrics and human judgment regarding LVLM faithfulness. Among all metrics, our metric FAITHSCORE ... The p-value of the significant test between our result and the baseline result is less than 0.01.
content: The proposed FAITHSCORE metric shows the highest correlation with human judgment of LVLM hallucinations among all evaluated metrics, with statistical significance (p < 0.01) compared to baselines.

statement: LLaVA-1.5 outperforms other evaluated LVLMs in most situations on both the LLaVA-1k and MSCOCO-Cap datasets when measured by FAITHSCORE, indicating superior faithfulness during generation.
reasoning: This fact synthesizes a key quantitative comparison across multiple models and datasets, directly supporting the paper's core claim about LLaVA-1.5's faithfulness. It is not obvious without reading the results and is central for researchers comparing LVLMs.
source_text: We observe that: (1) LLaVA-1.5 outperforms their counterparts in most situations. This demonstrates their preeminent capability in achieving and maintaining faithfulness during generation processes.
content: LLaVA-1.5 outperforms other evaluated LVLMs in most situations on both the LLaVA-1k and MSCOCO-Cap datasets when measured by FAITHSCORE, indicating superior faithfulness during generation.

statement: As the number of entities in answers increases on the LLaVA-1k dataset, model performance measured by FAITHSCORE drops significantly.
reasoning: This fact highlights a nuanced, quantitative relationship between answer complexity and model faithfulness, which is important for understanding model limitations and is not a background or setup detail.
source_text: Figure 4: The relation between FAITHSCORE and numbers of objects (i.e., entities) in the answers (LLaVA-1k dataset). As the number of entities increases, model performance (i.e., FAITHSCORE) drops significantly.
content: As the number of entities in answers increases on the LLaVA-1k dataset, model performance measured by FAITHSCORE drops significantly.

statement: LLaVA-1.5 achieves the highest overall FAITHSCORE (0.8566 on LLaVA-1k and 0.8258 on MSCOCO-Cap) among all evaluated LVLMs, outperforming InstructBLIP, mPLUG-Owl, MiniGPT-4, and Multimodal-GPT.
reasoning: This fact quantitatively compares multiple models on a novel metric (FAITHSCORE) across two datasets, highlighting LLaVA-1.5's superior performance, which is central to understanding model faithfulness.
source_text: LLaVA-1.5 0.8569 0.8611 0.8516 0.8566 0.9425 Table 4:   F AITH S CORE  evaluation results ( ↑ ) of different LVLMs on the LLaVA-1k and MSCOCO-Cap datasets... LLaVA-1.5 0.7722 0.7717 0.7699 0.7713 0.8258 Table 5:   F AITH S CORE  (sentence-level) evaluation results ( ↑ ) of different LVLMs.
content: LLaVA-1.5 achieves the highest overall FAITHSCORE (0.8566 on LLaVA-1k and 0.8258 on MSCOCO-Cap) among all evaluated LVLMs, outperforming InstructBLIP, mPLUG-Owl, MiniGPT-4, and Multimodal-GPT.

statement: The faithfulness of LVLMs, as measured by FAITHSCORE, generally decreases as the number of objects in the generated answer increases; for example, InstructBLIP's FAITHSCORE drops from 0.895 for 1 object to 0.662 for 10 objects.
reasoning: This fact reveals a nuanced, quantitative relationship between answer complexity (number of objects) and model faithfulness, which is important for understanding model limitations and guiding future improvements.
source_text: While all models start with relatively high scores when there are few objects in the answer, their performance generally drop as the number of objects increases. For example, Instruct-BLIP starts with a high F AITH S CORE  of 0.895 for 1 object and sustains a relatively low score of 0.662 for 10 objects.
content: The faithfulness of LVLMs, as measured by FAITHSCORE, generally decreases as the number of objects in the generated answer increases; for example, InstructBLIP's FAITHSCORE drops from 0.895 for 1 object to 0.662 for 10 objects.

statement: The FAITHSCORE metric focuses on factual precision by ensuring each piece of information in generated text is supported by the visual input, but it does not account for factual recall, meaning it does not penalize models for generating fewer facts.
reasoning: This fact is important for understanding the limitations of the FAITHSCORE metric, which is central to the paper's evaluation methodology. It requires interpreting the implications of how the metric operates and what aspects of model performance it does or does not capture.
source_text: FAITHSCORE focuses primarily on factual precision, ensuring that each piece of information in a generated text is supported by the visual input. However, it does not account for factual recall, meaning it doesn’t penalize models for generating fewer facts.
content: The FAITHSCORE metric focuses on factual precision by ensuring each piece of information in generated text is supported by the visual input, but it does not account for factual recall, meaning it does not penalize models for generating fewer facts.

Hallusionbench:   You see what you think?   or you think what you see?   an image-context reasoning benchmark challenging for gpt-4v (ision), llava-1.5, and   other   multi-modality   models.
content: The Hallusionbench benchmark is designed to challenge the reasoning abilities of large multi-modal models such as GPT-4V(ision) and LLaVA-1.5 by testing their image-context reasoning capabilities.

statement: On the LRV-Instruction dataset, the LLaVA-1.5 model achieved the highest human faithfulness score (0.7855) compared to InstructBLIP (0.6626), Multimodal-GPT (0.4903), mPLUG-Owl (0.6433), MiniGPT-4 (0.4638), and LLaVA (0.7017), as measured by a Likert-scale-based human evaluation of hallucinations in generated answers.
reasoning: This fact quantitatively compares multiple models on a specific dataset using a human-evaluated metric, highlighting LLaVA-1.5's superior performance. It requires interpreting the context of the metric and the dataset, making it more than a simple fact.
source_text: The results are shown as follows, which are consistent with the experimental results on datasets LLaVA-1k and MSCOCO-Cap: InstructBLIP 0.6626, Multimodal-GPT 0.4903, mPLUG-Owl 0.6433, MiniGPT-4 0.4638, LLaVA 0.7017, LLaVA-1.5 0.7855.
content: On the LRV-Instruction dataset, the LLaVA-1.5 model achieved the highest human faithfulness score (0.7855) compared to InstructBLIP (0.6626), Multimodal-GPT (0.4903), mPLUG-Owl (0.6433), MiniGPT-4 (0.4638), and LLaVA (0.7017), as measured by a Likert-scale-based human evaluation of hallucinations in generated answers.

statement: The FAITHSCORE metric achieves the highest correlation with human judgment on LVLM hallucinations, with Pearson’s r = 48.17%, Spearman’s ρ = 38.44%, and Kendall’s τ = 47.61%, outperforming all other evaluated metrics on the LLaVA dataset.
reasoning: This fact quantitatively compares the new metric (FAITHSCORE) to established metrics, showing it has the best alignment with human judgment, which is central to the paper's contribution.
source_text: Ours 48.17 38.44 47.61 Table 7: Correlation between each evaluation metric and human judgment on LVLM hallucinations, measured by Pearson’s  r , Spearman’s  ρ , and Kendall’s  τ . ... Amid the varied metrics landscape, our metric F AITH S CORE  achieved best correlation with human correlation.
content: The FAITHSCORE metric achieves the highest correlation with human judgment on LVLM hallucinations, with Pearson’s r = 48.17%, Spearman’s ρ = 38.44%, and Kendall’s τ = 47.61%, outperforming all other evaluated metrics on the LLaVA dataset.

statement: Traditional reference-based metrics such as BLEU, ROUGE, and METEOR show poor correlation with human evaluation of LVLM hallucinations on the LLaVA dataset, with Pearson’s r values ranging from -15.1% (BLEU-1) to -1.9% (BLEU-4).
reasoning: This fact highlights a key empirical finding that commonly used automatic metrics do not align well with human judgments in this context, motivating the need for improved evaluation methods.
source_text: BLEU-1 -15.1 ... BLEU-4 -1.9 ... ROUGE-1 -6.6 ... METEOR -12.2 ... Table 7: Correlation between each evaluation metric and human judgment on LVLM hallucinations, measured by Pearson’s  r ... Traditional metrics that require references ( i.e.,  BLEU, ROUGE, and ME- TEOR), have a poor correlation with human eval- uation.
content: Traditional reference-based metrics such as BLEU, ROUGE, and METEOR show poor correlation with human evaluation of LVLM hallucinations on the LLaVA dataset, with Pearson’s r values ranging from -15.1% (BLEU-1) to -1.9% (BLEU-4).

reasoning: This fact quantitatively compares the performance of closed-source and open-source models on a specific benchmark, highlighting the superiority of closed-source models. It requires interpreting tabular data and understanding the context of model evaluation.

content: On the LLaVA-1k benchmark, the closed-source models GPT-4V and Gemini achieve Pearson's r correlations of 0.8788 and 0.8865, respectively, surpassing all evaluated open-source models, with the best open-source model (LLaVA-1.5) achieving 0.8566.

statement: Atomic elements in data annotation are defined as minimal, self-contained pieces of information derived from image-related sub-sentences, such as entities, relations, colors, counting, and other attributes.

statement: An atomic element for the entity type must only contain one entity, and relations can be decomposed into two atomic elements of entity type.
reasoning: This fact requires understanding the decomposition rules for atomic elements, which is essential for correct annotation.
source_text: Entities: Only contain one entity. Relations: Only can be decomposed into two atomic elements of entity type.
content: An atomic element for the entity type must only contain one entity, and relations can be decomposed into two atomic elements of entity type.

statement: Color and counting atomic elements must refer to only one kind of entity.
reasoning: This is a direct rule for annotators to ensure atomicity and specificity in labeling.

statement: The EFUF (Efficient Fine-Grained Unlearning Framework) method eliminates hallucinations in multimodal large language models by performing gradient ascent with three tailored losses, and does so without requiring paired data.
reasoning: This fact captures the novel methodological contribution of the paper: a new framework (EFUF) that addresses hallucinations in MLLMs using a specific optimization approach and without the need for paired data, which is a key innovation over prior methods.
source_text: To address these issues, we propose an efficient fine-grained unlearning framework (EFUF), which performs gradient ascent utilizing three tailored losses to eliminate hallucinations without paired data.
content: The EFUF (Efficient Fine-Grained Unlearning Framework) method eliminates hallucinations in multimodal large language models by performing gradient ascent with three tailored losses, and does so without requiring paired data.

statement: Experiments show that the EFUF method consistently reduces hallucinations in multimodal large language models while preserving generation quality and incurring only modest computational overhead.
reasoning: This fact summarizes the main empirical finding: EFUF achieves its goal of reducing hallucinations without sacrificing output quality or efficiency, which is crucial for practical adoption and demonstrates the method's effectiveness.
source_text: Extensive experiments show that our method consistently reduces hallucinations while preserving the generation quality with modest computational overhead.
content: Experiments show that the EFUF method consistently reduces hallucinations in multimodal large language models while preserving generation quality and incurring only modest computational overhead.

statement: The Efficient Fine-Grained Unlearning Framework (EFUF) mitigates hallucinations in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) without requiring paired data and with lower computational resource consumption compared to traditional alignment algorithms.
reasoning: This fact synthesizes several methodological advantages of EFUF over existing approaches, specifically its independence from paired data and reduced computational cost, which are central to the paper's contribution.
source_text: To tackle the above issues, we propose the Efficient Fine-Grained Unlearning Framework (EFUF), which offers the advantage of not necessitating paired data and being more efficient during the finetuning phase. Our method, grounded in the principles of unlearning, mainly relies on performing gradient ascent on negative samples to mitigate hallucinations, eliminating the need for costly manually-annotated paired data. Additionally, it consumes considerably fewer computational resources. Unlike traditional alignment algorithms that require simultaneous operation of multiple models to execute preference alignment, EFUF operates without this requirement.
content: The Efficient Fine-Grained Unlearning Framework (EFUF) mitigates hallucinations in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) without requiring paired data and with lower computational resource consumption compared to traditional alignment algorithms.

statement: EFUF uses CLIP model scores to curate positive and negative examples for unlearning, enabling cost-effective and reliable identification of hallucinated versus real objects in MLLM outputs.
reasoning: This fact highlights the novel use of CLIP for dataset curation in the unlearning framework, which is a methodological innovation central to the paper's approach.
source_text: After ascertaining the capability of CLIP through a preliminary experiment, we curate our dataset manually-free by utilizing CLIP scores, before applying our unlearning-based method to MLLMs. ... The key to applying the unlearning algorithm is how to curate positive and negative samples, i.e., distinguish between real and hallucinated objects, in a manner that is both cost-effective and reliable. Intuitively, the similarity between objects and their corresponding images can act as an indicator for hallucinations, since the image contains real objects but not the hallucinated ones.
content: EFUF uses CLIP model scores to curate positive and negative examples for unlearning, enabling cost-effective and reliable identification of hallucinated versus real objects in MLLM outputs.

statement: The authors' methodology is the first to apply unlearning techniques specifically to multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for the targeted removal of hallucinated objects, rather than applying unlearning across the entire model output.
reasoning: This fact is meaningful because it highlights the novel methodological contribution of the paper: the targeted application of unlearning to hallucinated objects in MLLMs, which distinguishes this work from prior unlearning approaches that operate more broadly.
source_text: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt to adopt unlearning to multimodal large language models... In our research, we extend the concept of unlearning to the realm of multimodal hallucination mitigation in MLLMs, proposing a novel solution for enhancing model reliability and accuracy in multimodal contexts. In contrast to earlier approaches that apply unlearning across the entirety of a model’s responses, our methodology focuses exclusively on the unlearning of hallucinated objects.
content: The authors' methodology is the first to apply unlearning techniques specifically to multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for the targeted removal of hallucinated objects, rather than applying unlearning across the entire model output.

statement: In a preliminary experiment, the authors manually annotated 200 image captions generated by MiniGPT and LLaVA, labeling objects as hallucinated or non-hallucinated, and used fine-grained CLIP similarity scores to investigate differences in image-text congruence between these groups.
reasoning: This fact is meaningful because it describes the core experimental setup used to validate the hypothesis that CLIP similarity can distinguish hallucinated from non-hallucinated objects, which underpins the paper's methodology.
source_text: Following Zhou et al. (2023), we manually annotate 200 image captions generated by MiniGPT (Zhu et al., 2023) and LLaVA (Liu et al., 2023b), labeling objects as either hallucinated or non-hallucinated. Subsequently, we define an object-level image-relevance score by calculating fine-grained CLIP similarities for these objects in relation to their associated image segments, aiming to uncover any significant disparities in score distributions.
content: In a preliminary experiment, the authors manually annotated 200 image captions generated by MiniGPT and LLaVA, labeling objects as hallucinated or non-hallucinated, and used fine-grained CLIP similarity scores to investigate differences in image-text congruence between these groups.

statement: For both MiniGPT4 and LLaVA, hallucinated objects have significantly lower mean image-relevance scores (25.35 and 26.11, respectively) compared to non-hallucinated objects (28.26 and 28.64), with p-values of 6.0×10⁻³⁰ and 2.5×10⁻¹² indicating strong statistical significance.
reasoning: This fact quantitatively demonstrates a core finding: image-relevance scores (using CLIP) can distinguish hallucinated from non-hallucinated objects, with strong statistical support. This underpins the methodology for hallucination detection and is central to the paper's contribution.
source_text: Model Hal. Mean Std. p MiniGPT4 No 28.26 2.74 6 . 0  ×  10 − 30 Yes 25.35 2.70 LLaVA No 28.64 2.65 2 . 5  ×  10 − 12 Yes 26.11 2.27 Table   1:   Statistics   and   significance   test   on   samples generated   by   MiniGPT4   and   LLaVA. ... This statisti- cal evidence allows us to confidently reject the null hypothesis that the two distributions have identical means, underscoring the utility of CLIP similarity scores in detecting hallucinations.
content: For both MiniGPT4 and LLaVA, hallucinated objects have significantly lower mean image-relevance scores (25.35 and 26.11, respectively) compared to non-hallucinated objects (28.26 and 28.64), with p-values of 6.0×10⁻³⁰ and 2.5×10⁻¹² indicating strong statistical significance.

statement: The EFUF method constructs three datasets—positive subsentence, negative subsentence, and complete sentence—by extracting objects from generated captions and calculating their image relevance using CLIP, enabling fine-grained unlearning of multimodal hallucinations without manual annotation.
reasoning: This fact synthesizes the novel methodological approach of EFUF, highlighting the use of CLIP-based relevance scoring and thresholding to automate dataset creation for unlearning, which is central to the paper's contribution.
source_text: Initially, we extract objects from generated captions and calculate their image relevance utilizing CLIP, followed by the construction of three datasets. ... By setting thresholds for the scores, we are able to discern and compile distinct samples from the generated text, forming a dataset for fine-tuning that circumvents the need for labor-intensive manual annotation.
content: The EFUF method constructs three datasets—positive subsentence, negative subsentence, and complete sentence—by extracting objects from generated captions and calculating their image relevance using CLIP, enabling fine-grained unlearning of multimodal hallucinations without manual annotation.

statement: The unlearning process in EFUF employs three distinct loss functions—sentence loss, positive loss, and negative loss—designed to discard incorrect multimodal alignments that cause hallucinations while preserving correct alignments, and generally requires less computational resources than conventional alignment algorithms during finetuning.
reasoning: This fact captures the core methodological innovation (the three-loss unlearning framework) and its practical advantage (lower computational cost), both of which are key for understanding the paper's contribution.
source_text: During the finetuning phase, we employ an efficient unlearning method, which involves the development of three distinct types of losses. These losses are designed to aid the model in discarding incorrect multimodal alignments that could lead to hallucinations, while preserving the correct alignments essential for tasks. Unlearning generally requires less computation resources compared with conventional alignment algorithms in the finetuning stage, so the computation amount can also be effectively reduced.
content: The unlearning process in EFUF employs three distinct loss functions—sentence loss, positive loss, and negative loss—designed to discard incorrect multimodal alignments that cause hallucinations while preserving correct alignments, and generally requires less computational resources than conventional alignment algorithms during finetuning.

statement: The proposed unlearning approach for multimodal large language models combines three losses: a negative loss applied via gradient ascent to subsentences with hallucinated objects, a positive loss to subsentences with non-hallucinated objects, and a sentence loss to preserve the ability to generate coherent long-form text.
reasoning: This fact synthesizes the core methodological innovation: the combination of targeted negative and positive losses at the subsentence level, plus a sentence-level loss, which is central to the paper's approach for reducing hallucinations while maintaining text quality.
source_text: Thus, we introduce a dual-faceted fine-grained unlearn- ing approach: applying a negative loss to the sub- sentences containing hallucinated objects, and a positive loss to those containing non-hallucinated objects. This strategy aims to curtail the production of hallucinated content while encouraging precise object representation, thus diminishing the occur- rence of hallucinations. Meanwhile, we also pro- pose a sentence loss, aiming to preserve the model’s ability to generate cohesive, long-form text.
content: The proposed unlearning approach for multimodal large language models combines three losses: a negative loss applied via gradient ascent to subsentences with hallucinated objects, a positive loss to subsentences with non-hallucinated objects, and a sentence loss to preserve the ability to generate coherent long-form text.

statement: The unlearning dataset for the proposed method consists of three subsets: D+ (positive samples), D− (negative samples), and Ds (filtered responses with high image-relevance scores), and the overall loss is a weighted sum of the positive, negative, and sentence losses.
reasoning: This fact details the construction of the unlearning dataset and the formulation of the final loss function, which are crucial for understanding how the method operationalizes its training and unlearning objectives.
source_text: Finally, we take D unlearning = { D + , D − , D s } as our unlearning dataset... The overall loss equation then becomes a weighted amalgamation of these three components: L = L pos + λ 1 L neg + λ 2 L sent , (8) where λ 1 and λ 2 represent the unlearning weight and the sentence weight respectively.
content: The unlearning dataset for the proposed method consists of three subsets: D+ (positive samples), D− (negative samples), and Ds (filtered responses with high image-relevance scores), and the overall loss is a weighted sum of the positive, negative, and sentence losses.

statement: Integrating EFUF into four state-of-the-art MLLMs (MiniGPT4, LLaVA, mPLUG-owl, ShareGPT4V) reduces hallucination rates by approximately 15% (Chair S), 5% (Chair I), 18% (Human S), 8% (Human I), and 1% (POPE) on average compared to the base models.
reasoning: This fact synthesizes quantitative improvements across multiple metrics and models, showing the generalizability and effectiveness of EFUF in reducing hallucination, which is central to the paper's contribution.
source_text: our approach demonstrates a consistent reduction in hallucination rates across all four MLLMs, with an average improvement of approximately 15% and 5% on the Chair S and Chair I metric, 18% and 8% on the Human S and Human I metric, and 1% on the POPE metric.
content: Integrating EFUF into four state-of-the-art MLLMs (MiniGPT4, LLaVA, mPLUG-owl, ShareGPT4V) reduces hallucination rates by approximately 15% (Chair S), 5% (Chair I), 18% (Human S), 8% (Human I), and 1% (POPE) on average compared to the base models.

statement: Applying EFUF to four MLLMs improves generation quality, increasing BLEU-1 by 4%, BLEU-2 by 3%, BLEU-4 by 2%, informativeness by 1%, and fluency by 1% on average compared to the base models.
reasoning: This fact captures the quantitative impact of EFUF on multiple aspects of generation quality, highlighting its broad benefit beyond hallucination reduction, which is important for understanding the method's overall value.
source_text: Specifically, it improves BLEU-1 by 4%, BLEU-2 by 3%, BLEU-4 by 2%, informativeness by 1%, and fluency by 1%, across the four models.
content: Applying EFUF to four MLLMs improves generation quality, increasing BLEU-1 by 4%, BLEU-2 by 3%, BLEU-4 by 2%, informativeness by 1%, and fluency by 1% on average compared to the base models.

statement: The EFUF method achieves a hallucination rate of 41.9% and a BLEU-4 score of 16.8 on the LLaVA model, outperforming RLHF (hallucination rate 60.2%, BLEU-4 12.6), HADPO (hallucination rate 52.3%, BLEU-4 15.7), and POVID (hallucination rate 41.3%, BLEU-4 15.1), while maintaining comparable or superior generation quality with lower data and training costs.
reasoning: This fact synthesizes quantitative results from Table 4 and contextualizes them with the text's claim about EFUF's efficiency and performance relative to other methods, which is crucial for understanding EFUF's practical advantages.
source_text: We measure EFUF’s generation quality along with hallucination rate in Table 4. Compared to other hallucination mitigation methods, EFUF demonstrates comparable or superior performance, while requiring minimal data construction cost and training resources among all. Additionally, our improvements in generation quality are on par with RLHF-based methods, which typically demand expensive human...
content: The EFUF method achieves a hallucination rate of 41.9% and a BLEU-4 score of 16.8 on the LLaVA model, outperforming RLHF (hallucination rate 60.2%, BLEU-4 12.6), HADPO (hallucination rate 52.3%, BLEU-4 15.7), and POVID (hallucination rate 41.3%, BLEU-4 15.1), while maintaining comparable or superior generation quality with lower data and training costs.

statement: Fine-grained unlearning achieves the lowest hallucination rate (e.g., 36.1% on MiniGPT4), but this comes at the cost of significantly reduced fluency and informativeness, as indicated by increased perplexity (0.170) and manual examination showing fragmented, incoherent sentences.
reasoning: This fact combines quantitative and qualitative evidence to highlight a key trade-off in hallucination mitigation: reducing hallucinations via fine-grained unlearning can harm output fluency, which is a nuanced and important finding for researchers.
source_text: Although fine-grained unlearning achieves the lowest hallucination rate, it drastically sacrifices fluency, making the generated content difficult for humans to read... The decline in fluency is highlighted by a significant increase in perplexity, rendering the responses largely unreadable by humans. Manual examination further reveals that the generated content often consists fragmented and incoherent sentences.
content: Fine-grained unlearning achieves the lowest hallucination rate (e.g., 36.1% on MiniGPT4), but this comes at the cost of significantly reduced fluency and informativeness, as indicated by increased perplexity (0.170) and manual examination showing fragmented, incoherent sentences.

statement: The EFUF method requires only 3 GPU hours for training the MiniGPT4 model, which is significantly less than RLHF-based finetuning (20 GPU hours), DPO (8-16 GPU hours), and contrastive learning (10 GPU hours) on comparable tasks.
reasoning: This fact quantitatively compares the resource efficiency of EFUF to several established methods, highlighting a core advantage of the proposed approach. It requires understanding the context of GPU hour usage and the significance of such a reduction.
source_text: MiniGPT4 model requires merely 3 GPU hours, a fraction of the resources needed by other methods. For comparison, RLHF-based finetuning typically consumes 20 GPU hours ( Sun et al. ,  2023 ), DPO ranges from 8 ( Yu et al. ,  2023 ) to 16 ( Zhao et al. , 2023 ) GPU hours, and contrastive learning method requires around 10 GPU hours ( Jiang et al. ,  2023 ).
content: The EFUF method requires only 3 GPU hours for training the MiniGPT4 model, which is significantly less than RLHF-based finetuning (20 GPU hours), DPO (8-16 GPU hours), and contrastive learning (10 GPU hours) on comparable tasks.

statement: The investigation in this paper primarily addresses object hallucinations in multimodal large language models, measured by the presence or absence of the depicted object in the corresponding image.
reasoning: This fact is meaningful because it clarifies the specific type of hallucination the paper targets (object hallucination), and the operational definition used (presence/absence in the image), which is crucial for understanding the scope and evaluation methodology of the research.
source_text: our investigation primarily addresses object hallucina- tions,   gauged   by   the   presence   or   absence   of   the depicted object in the corresponding image.
content: The investigation in this paper primarily addresses object hallucinations in multimodal large language models, measured by the presence or absence of the depicted object in the corresponding image.

statement: The paper 'Mitigating hallucinations in large vision-language models with instruction contrastive decoding' (Wang et al., 2024) proposes a method specifically designed to reduce hallucinations in large vision-language models.
reasoning: This fact is meaningful because it identifies a novel methodological contribution (instruction contrastive decoding) aimed at a key challenge (hallucinations) in vision-language models, which is central to understanding advancements in this research area.
source_text: Xintong Wang, Jingheng Pan, Liang Ding, and Chris Biemann. 2024. Mitigating hallucinations in large vision-language models with instruction contrastive decoding.
content: The paper 'Mitigating hallucinations in large vision-language models with instruction contrastive decoding' (Wang et al., 2024) proposes a method specifically designed to reduce hallucinations in large vision-language models.

statement: The evaluation of object hallucination in model-generated content uses three main metrics: CHAIR (measuring the ratio of non-existent objects referenced to all mentioned objects at both sentence and instance levels), MHumanEval (human expert review of a random subset of 100 responses for hallucinated and non-hallucinated objects), and POPE (F1 scores based on automated segmentation and model queries about object presence, including random non-existent objects).
reasoning: This fact synthesizes the core methodological approach for evaluating hallucination, specifying the three distinct metrics and their operationalization, which is crucial for understanding how the paper measures its main outcome.
source_text: To quantify the rate of hallucinations, we utilize CHAIR (Rohrbach et al., 2018) and MHumanEval (Yu et al., 2023), which allow us to measure hallucinations at both the sentence and instance levels for model-generated content. Additionally, POPE (Fu et al., 2023) is incorporated into our evaluation to directly assess the models via VQA. ... (1) CHAIR. ... It quantifies hallucination by calculating the ratio of non-existent objects referenced in the model’s response to the total number of objects mentioned. ... (2) MHumanEval. Recognizing the limitations of CHAIR in covering only a set of pre-defined object categories, we also incorporate human judgment into our evaluation. Following (Yu et al., 2023), we select a random subset of 100 responses for expert review to identify hallucinated and non-hallucinated objects. ... (3) POPE. ... POPE leverages an automated segmentation tool to delineate objects within images, subsequently querying the model regarding their presence, as well as introducing random non-existent objects. We present the F1 scores, offering insights into the model’s image perception capabilities.
content: The evaluation of object hallucination in model-generated content uses three main metrics: CHAIR (measuring the ratio of non-existent objects referenced to all mentioned objects at both sentence and instance levels), MHumanEval (human expert review of a random subset of 100 responses for hallucinated and non-hallucinated objects), and POPE (F1 scores based on automated segmentation and model queries about object presence, including random non-existent objects).

statement: When training the ShareGPT4V model with the EFUF strategy, increasing the negative loss weight λ1 from 0.1 to 0.3 reduces hallucination rates and improves generation quality, but further increases beyond 0.3 (especially at 0.4) continue to reduce hallucinations at the cost of degraded informativeness and fluency; thus, λ1 = 0.3 is identified as the optimal balance point.
reasoning: This fact synthesizes the nuanced relationship between the negative loss weight and multiple performance metrics, identifying a specific optimal value based on empirical trends. It is important for researchers tuning similar models and is not a trivial or obvious claim.
source_text: as λ1 is incremented from 0.1 to 0.4, we initially note enhancements in both hallucination reduction and generation quality metrics, up until a value of 0.2. Beyond this threshold and past the value of 0.3, a new trend emerges: while the rate of hallucinations continues to decline, a noticeable degradation in generation quality become apparent. ... a value of 0.3 for λ1 is identified as the optimal balance point, effectively minimizing hallucinations without compromising the integrity of generation quality.
content: When training the ShareGPT4V model with the EFUF strategy, increasing the negative loss weight λ1 from 0.1 to 0.3 reduces hallucination rates and improves generation quality, but further increases beyond 0.3 (especially at 0.4) continue to reduce hallucinations at the cost of degraded informativeness and fluency; thus, λ1 = 0.3 is identified as the optimal balance point.

reasoning: This fact highlights the compatibility and additive effect of EFUF with other advanced hallucination mitigation techniques, which is a key methodological finding relevant for practitioners seeking to stack improvements.
source_text: Results, detailed in Table 6, indicate a notable reduction in hallucination rates post-EFUF application, without compromising the quality of the generated text. This outcome underscores EFUF’s value as an additive method, capable of augmenting the perfor-
content: Applying the EFUF method to models already enhanced with RLHF (LLaVA-RLHF) or instruction tuning (LRV) results in a notable reduction in hallucination rates without compromising generated text quality, demonstrating EFUF's additive benefit to existing hallucination mitigation strategies.

statement: Adding the EFUF method to the LRV model reduces the hallucination rate from 39.4% to 37.3% and improves Bleu1 from 51.8 to 51.2, as shown in Table 6.
reasoning: This fact synthesizes quantitative results from Table 6, showing the effect of EFUF on hallucination rate and Bleu1 score when added to LRV, which is central to evaluating EFUF's effectiveness.
source_text: LRV 39.4 19.9 46.0 16.0 85.1 51.8 36.6 20.5 88.4 0.129 +  EFUF 37.3 19.5 45.0 15.1 85.1 51.2 36.3 20.7 87.7 0.118 Table 6:   Performance comparison of EFUF added on other hallucination mitigating approaches (%).
content: Adding the EFUF method to the LRV model reduces the hallucination rate from 39.4% to 37.3% and improves Bleu1 from 51.8 to 51.2, as shown in Table 6.

statement: The EFUF approach, by integrating a fine-grained unlearning strategy, enables the model to avoid generating hallucinated objects in multimodal outputs, outperforming both the baseline MiniGPT4 and sentence-loss-only methods in a case study.
reasoning: This fact captures the core methodological contribution of EFUF and its qualitative advantage over other approaches, which is key for understanding the paper's novelty and impact.
source_text: In   contrast,   our   EFUF   approach   successfully addresses   this   challenge.   By   integrating   a   fine- grained unlearning strategy, EFUF effectively dis- courages   the   generation   of   objects   with   low   rel- evance   to   the   given   image.   This   direct   interven- tion   ensures   that   the   model   refrains   from   includ- ing hallucinated objects in its outputs, showcasing a   significant   improvement   over   the   baseline   and sentence-loss-only method.
content: The EFUF approach, by integrating a fine-grained unlearning strategy, enables the model to avoid generating hallucinated objects in multimodal outputs, outperforming both the baseline MiniGPT4 and sentence-loss-only methods in a case study.

statement: The AUTOHALLUSION benchmark generation approach uses three automated image manipulation strategies—abnormal object insertion, paired object insertion, and correlated object removal—to induce hallucinations in large vision-language models (LVLMs).
reasoning: This fact summarizes the novel methodological contribution of the paper, specifying the three strategies used to systematically generate hallucination-inducing examples, which is central to the paper's approach.
source_text: We propose three image manipulation strategies to induce hallucinations: abnormal object insertion, paired object insertion, and correlated object removal, which trigger the conflicts between the images and LVLM priors.
content: The AUTOHALLUSION benchmark generation approach uses three automated image manipulation strategies—abnormal object insertion, paired object insertion, and correlated object removal—to induce hallucinations in large vision-language models (LVLMs).

statement: Comprehensive evaluations show that AUTOHALLUSION achieves a 97.7% and 98.7% success rate of hallucination induction on synthetic and real-world datasets, respectively, when tested on top-tier LVLMs such as GPT-4V(ision), Gemini Pro Vision, Claude 3, and LLaVA-1.5.
reasoning: This fact provides a quantitative result demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed benchmark in reliably inducing hallucinations across multiple state-of-the-art models, which is a key finding for researchers evaluating LVLM robustness.
source_text: Comprehensive evaluations of top-tier LVLMs, e.g., GPT-4V(ision), Gemini Pro Vision, Claude 3, and LLaVA-1.5, show a 97.7% and 98.7% success rate of hallucination induction on synthetic and real-world datasets of AUTOHALLUSION, paving the way for a long battle against hallucinations.
content: Comprehensive evaluations show that AUTOHALLUSION achieves a 97.7% and 98.7% success rate of hallucination induction on synthetic and real-world datasets, respectively, when tested on top-tier LVLMs such as GPT-4V(ision), Gemini Pro Vision, Claude 3, and LLaVA-1.5.

statement: The AUTOHALLUSION method achieves success rates of 97.7% and 98.7% in inducing hallucinations in state-of-the-art LVLMs on synthetic and real-world data, respectively.
reasoning: This fact quantitatively demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed method (AUTOHALLUSION) in reliably inducing hallucinations in leading LVLMs, which is central to the paper's contribution and not common knowledge.
